en.extract:
The wallcreeper (Tichodroma muraria) is a small passerine bird found throughout the high mountains of the Palearctic from southern Europe to central China. It is the only extant member of both the genus Tichodroma and the family Tichodromidae.


Taxonomy and systematics
In the past, there was some disagreement among ornithologists as to where the wallcreeper belongs in the taxonomic order. Initially, Linnaeus included it in the treecreepers as Certhia muraria, and even when given a separate genus of its own, Tichodroma, by Johann Karl Wilhelm Illiger in 1811, it was long included in the treecreeper family Certhiidae. More recently, it was placed in its own monotypic family, Tichodromadidae, by Karel Voous in the influential List of Recent Holarctic Bird Species, while other authorities such as Charles Vaurie put it in a monotypic family called Tichodromadinae, as a subfamily of the nuthatch family Sittidae. In either case, it is closely related to the nuthatches; a 2016 phylogenetic study of members in the superfamily Certhioidea suggests it is a sister species to the Sittidae. At least one other species of wallcreeper is known from the fossil record, Tichodroma capeki (Late Miocene of Polgardi, Hungary).

The genus name Tichodroma comes from the Ancient Greek teikhos, meaning "wall", and dromos, meaning "runner". The specific name muraria is Medieval Latin for "of walls", from Latin murus, "wall". Alternatively, the wallcreeper is named the red-winged wall creeper.


Subspecies
Two subspecies are accepted:

European wallcreeper (T. m. muraria) - (Linnaeus, 1766): Found from southern and eastern Europe to the Caucasus and western Iran
Asian wallcreeper (T. m. nepalensis) - Bonaparte, 1850: Originally described as a separate species. Found from Kazakhstan, Turkmenistan and eastern Iran to eastern China


Description
The wallcreeper is 15.5–17 centimetres (6.1–6.7 in) long, with a weight of 17–19 grams (0.60–0.67 oz). Its plumage is primarily blue-grey, with darker flight and tail feathers. In summer, the males have a black throat grading into the grey of the rest of the body, and females can have either a white throat or a small dark patch on the throat; in autumn and winter, both sexes have a white throat. Its most striking plumage feature, though, are its extraordinary crimson wings with white spots.  Largely hidden when the wings are folded, this bright colouring covers most of the covert feathers, and the basal half of the primaries and secondaries. The tail is short, black with a narrow white fringe. Juveniles closely resemble the winter plumage. The subspecies T. m. nepalensis is slightly darker than the nominate race.


Vocalisations
Though largely silent, both male and female wallcreepers sing, the females generally only while defending feeding territories in the winter.  The song is a high-pitched, drawn-out whistle, with notes that alternately rise and fall.  During the breeding season, the male sings while perched or climbing.


Distribution and habitat

A bird of high mountains, the wallcreeper breeds at elevations ranging between 1,000 and 3,000 metres (3,300 and 9,800 ft) in Europe, between 2,800 and 4,000 metres (9,200 and 13,100 ft) in the Tien Shan, and 3,600 and 5,100 metres (11,800 and 16,700 ft) in the Himalaya. It is largely resident across its range, but moves to lower elevations in winter, when it is found on buildings and in quarries. In France it regularly and repeatedly winters on cathedrals and viaducts in Brittany and Normandy. Birds have wintered as far afield as England and the Netherlands, where one spent two consecutive winters between 1989 and 1991 at the Vrije Universiteit in Amsterdam. The species is resident across much of the Himalayas, ranging across India, Nepal, Bhutan and parts of Tibet and also as a winter visitor in Bangladesh. It's endemic to Eurasian mountains.


Behaviour and ecology

This species can be quite tame, but is often surprisingly difficult to see on mountain faces. While it may be confiding in the breeding and non-breeding seasons, and vagrant birds especially are extremely tame, they will still hide when they are aware of being watched, and will hesitate before entering the nest and even take roundabout routes towards the nest during prolonged observations.
Wallcreepers are territorial, and pairs vigorously defend their breeding territory during the summer. During the winter the wallcreeper is solitary, with males and females defending individual feeding territories. The size of these feeding territories is hard to estimate but may comprise a single large quarry or rock massif; or, alternatively, a series of smaller quarries and rock faces. Wallcreepers may travel some distances from roosting sites to feeding territories. They have also been demonstrated showing site fidelity to winter feeding territories in consecutive years.


Breeding

The female wallcreeper builds a cup nest of grass and moss, sheltered deep in a rock crevice, hole or cave. The nest is lined with softer materials, often including feathers or wool, and typically has two entrances. The female usually lays 4–5 eggs, though clutches as small as three have been found.  The white eggs measure 21 mm long, and are marked with a small number of black or reddish-brown speckles.  Once her entire clutch has been laid, the female incubates the eggs for 19–20 days, until they hatch. During incubation, she is regularly fed by her mate. Young are altricial, which means they are blind, featherless and helpless at birth. Both parents feed the nestlings for a period of 28–30 days, until the young birds fledge.  Each pair raises a single brood a year.


Feeding
The wallcreeper is an insectivore, feeding on terrestrial invertebrates, primarily insects and spiders, gleaned from rock faces.  It sometimes also chases flying insects in short sallies from a rock wall perch. Feeding birds move across a cliff face in short flights and quick hops, often with their wings partially spread.


References


External links
 Media related to Tichodroma muraria at Wikimedia Commons
 Data related to Tichodroma muraria at Wikispecies
Tichodroma muraria in Field Guide: Birds of the World on Flickr
Neveu Frank, Salamandre Films. (ed) 2013. Like a butterfly - The incredible life of the wallcreeper

The '''wallcreeper''' ('''''Tichodroma muraria''''') is a small [[passerine]] [[bird]] found throughout the high mountains of the [[Palearctic]] from southern Europe to central [[China]]. It is the only extant member of both the [[genus]] ''[[Tichodroma]]'' and the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] Tichodromidae.

==Taxonomy and systematics==
In the past, there was some disagreement among [[ornithologist]]s as to where the wallcreeper belongs in the taxonomic order. Initially, [[Carl Linnaeus|Linnaeus]] included it in the [[treecreeper]]s as ''Certhia muraria'',<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Linnaeus |first1=Carolus |title=ed. 12 |journal=Systemae Naturae |date=1766 |volume=1 |issue=1 |pages=184–185 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/42946380 |access-date=6 October 2024}}</ref><ref name=Lack>{{cite book |title=A Dictionary of Birds |last=Campbell |first=Bruce |author2=Elizabeth Lack |year=1985 |author2-link=Elizabeth Lack |publisher=T & A D Poyser |location=Calton |pages=638–39 |isbn=0-85661-039-9}}</ref> and even when given a separate genus of its own, ''Tichodroma'', by [[Johann Karl Wilhelm Illiger]] in 1811, it was long included in the treecreeper family [[Certhiidae]]. More recently, it was placed in its own monotypic family, Tichodromadidae, by [[Karel Voous]] in the influential ''[[List of Recent Holarctic Bird Species]]'',<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Voous |first1=Karel |title=List of Recent Holarctic Bird Species |journal=Ibis |date=1977 |volume=119 |page=379}}</ref> while other authorities such as [[Charles Vaurie]] put it in a monotypic family called Tichodromadinae, as a subfamily of the [[nuthatch]] family Sittidae.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Vaurie |first1=Charles |title=Systematic notes on palearctic birds. No 29. |journal=American Museum Novitates |date=1957 |volume=1854 |pages=2–6}}</ref> In either case, it is closely related to the nuthatches; a 2016 phylogenetic study of members in the superfamily [[Certhioidea]] suggests it is a sister species to the Sittidae.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1007/s10336-016-1340-8|title=Phylogenetic position of the Wallcreeper Tichodroma muraria|journal=Journal of Ornithology|volume=157|issue=3|page=913|year=2016|last1=Zhao|first1=Min|last2=Alström|first2=Per|last3=Olsson|first3=Urban|last4=Qu|first4=Yanhua|last5=Lei|first5=Fumin|s2cid=26838254}}</ref> At least one other species of wallcreeper is known from the fossil record, ''Tichodroma capeki'' (Late Miocene of Polgardi, Hungary).<ref>Kessler, E. 2013. Neogene songbirds (Aves, Passeriformes) from Hungary. – Hantkeniana,  Budapest, 2013, 8: 37-149.</ref>
{{Phylogeny/Certhioidea}}
The genus name ''Tichodroma'' comes from the [[Ancient Greek]] ''teikhos'', meaning "wall", and ''dromos'', meaning "runner". The specific name ''muraria'' is [[Medieval Latin]] for "of walls", from [[Latin]] ''murus'', "wall".<ref name=job>{{cite book | last= Jobling | first= James A | year= 2010| title= The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher= Christopher Helm | location = London | isbn = 978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages = 262, 385}}</ref> Alternatively, the wallcreeper is named the '''red-winged wall creeper'''.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=89C425901C5B5343|title=Tichodroma muraria (Wallcreeper) - Avibase|website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org|access-date=2017-07-23}}</ref>

===Subspecies===
Two subspecies are accepted:<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/nuthatch/|title=Nuthatches, Wallcreeper, treecreepers, mockingbirds, starlings & oxpeckers « IOC World Bird List|website=www.worldbirdnames.org|language=en-US|access-date=2017-07-23}}</ref>
* '''European wallcreeper''' (''T. m. muraria'') - <small>([[Carl Linnaeus|Linnaeus]], 1766)</small>: Found from southern and eastern Europe to the Caucasus and western Iran
* '''Asian wallcreeper''' (''T. m. nepalensis'') - <small>[[Charles Lucien Bonaparte|Bonaparte]], 1850</small>: Originally described as a separate species. Found from Kazakhstan, Turkmenistan and eastern Iran to eastern China

==Description==
The wallcreeper is {{convert|15.5|–|17|cm|in}} long, with a weight of {{convert|17|–|19|g|oz}}. Its {{birdgloss|plumage}} is primarily blue-grey, with darker flight and tail feathers. In summer, the males have a black throat grading into the grey of the rest of the body, and females can have either a white throat or a small dark patch on the throat; in autumn and winter, both sexes have a white throat. Its most striking plumage feature, though, are its extraordinary crimson wings with white spots.  Largely hidden when the wings are folded, this bright colouring covers most of the covert feathers, and the basal half of the primaries and secondaries. The tail is short, black with a narrow white fringe. Juveniles closely resemble the winter plumage. The subspecies ''T. m. nepalensis'' is slightly darker than the nominate race.

<gallery mode = packed heights = 180px>
Wallcreeper (Tichodroma muraria) female Piatra Craiului 2.jpg|Typical position at rest
Wallcreeper (Tichodroma muraria) female Piatra Craiului 4.jpg|Showing wing colour
File:Picchio Muraiolo in volo.jpg|In flight, showing the startling wing pattern
Wallcreeper (Tichodroma muraria) female Piatra Craiului.jpg|Female in the [[Piatra Craiului Mountains]], [[Transylvania]], Romania
</gallery>

===Vocalisations===
Though largely silent, both male and female wallcreepers [[bird call|sing]], the females generally only while defending feeding territories in the winter.<ref name=Handbook/>  The song is a high-pitched, drawn-out whistle, with notes that alternately rise and fall.<ref name=Collins/>  During the breeding season, the male sings while perched or climbing.

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Wallcreeper (Tichodroma muraria) (27844633078) (cropped).jpg|left|thumb|''T. m. nepalensis'', at [[Gojal]], [[Gilgit-Baltistan]], Pakistan]]
A bird of high mountains, the wallcreeper breeds at elevations ranging between {{convert|1000|and|3000|m|ft}} in Europe,<ref name=Collins>{{cite book |title=Collins Bird Guide |last=Svensson |first=Lars |author2=Peter J. Grant |year=1999 |publisher=HarperCollins |location=London |pages=324–5 }}</ref> between {{convert|2800|and|4000|m|ft}} in the [[Tien Shan]], and {{convert|3600|and|5100|m|ft}} in the [[Himalaya]].<ref name = "HBW">{{Citation | first = Simon | last = Harrap | editor-first = Josep | editor-last = del Hoyo | editor2-first = Andrew | editor2-last = Elliott | editor3-first = David | editor3-last = Christie | contribution = Family Tichodromidae (Wallcreeper) | title = [[Handbook of the Birds of the World]]. Volume 13, Penduline-tits to Shrikes | year = 2008 | pages = 146–165| place = Barcelona | publisher = Lynx Edicions | isbn = 978-84-96553-45-3 }}</ref> It is largely resident across its [[range (biology)|range]], but moves to lower elevations in winter, when it is found on buildings and in quarries. In France it regularly and repeatedly winters on cathedrals and viaducts in Brittany and Normandy.<ref>[https://www.ornithomedia.com/magazine/analyses/tichodrome-echelette-amateur-patrimoine-culturel-francais-00592/ ornithomedia.com] (in French)</ref> Birds have wintered as far afield as England and the Netherlands, where one spent two consecutive winters between 1989 and 1991 at the [[Vrije Universiteit]] in [[Amsterdam]].<ref>[http://waarneming.nl/soort/view/1576?from=1900-01-01&to=2010-01-14 waarneming.nl] (in Dutch)</ref> The species is resident across much of the [[Himalayas]], ranging across India, Nepal, Bhutan and parts of [[Tibet]] and also as a winter visitor in Bangladesh.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/22711234 | title=Wallcreeper (Tichodroma muraria) - BirdLife species factsheet }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|url=http://www.hbw.com/species/wallcreeper-tichodroma-muraria|title=Wallcreeper (Tichodroma muraria)|journal=Birds of the World|date=4 March 2020|last1=Kirwan|first1=Guy M.|last2=Löhrl|first2=Hans|last3=Wilson|first3=Mike|editor1-first=Shawn M|editor1-last=Billerman|editor2-first=Brooke K|editor2-last=Keeney|editor3-first=Paul G|editor3-last=Rodewald|editor4-first=Thomas S|editor4-last=Schulenberg|doi=10.2173/bow.wallcr1.01|s2cid=216231765|url-access=subscription}}</ref> It's [[Endemism|endemic]] to Eurasian mountains.

==Behaviour and ecology==
[[File:Wallcreeper (Tichodroma muraria) female composite Piatra Craiului.jpg|thumb|Dropping off a cliff edge]]
This species can be quite tame, but is often surprisingly difficult to see on mountain faces. While it may be confiding in the breeding and non-breeding seasons, and vagrant birds especially are extremely tame, they will still hide when they are aware of being watched, and will hesitate before entering the nest and even take roundabout routes towards the nest during prolonged observations.<ref name = "HBW"/>

Wallcreepers are territorial, and pairs vigorously defend their breeding territory during the summer. During the winter the wallcreeper is solitary, with males and females defending individual feeding territories. The size of these feeding territories is hard to estimate but may comprise a single large quarry or rock massif; or, alternatively, a series of smaller quarries and rock faces. Wallcreepers may travel some distances from roosting sites to feeding territories. They have also been demonstrated showing site fidelity to winter feeding territories in consecutive years.<ref name=HBW/>

===Breeding===
[[File:Tichodroma muraria  MHNT.ZOO.2010.11.184.1.jpg|thumb|''Tichodroma muraria'' eggs, [[MHNT]].]]
The female wallcreeper builds a [[bird nest|cup nest]] of grass and moss, sheltered deep in a rock crevice, hole or cave.<ref name=Handbook/> The nest is lined with softer materials, often including feathers or wool,<ref name=Lack/> and typically has two entrances. The female usually lays 4–5 [[bird egg|eggs]], though [[clutch (eggs)|clutch]]es as small as three have been found.  The white eggs measure 21 mm long, and are marked with a small number of black or reddish-brown speckles.  Once her entire clutch has been laid, the female [[avian incubation|incubates]] the eggs for 19–20 days, until they hatch. During incubation, she is regularly fed by her mate.<ref name=Lack/> Young are [[altricial]], which means they are blind, featherless and helpless at birth. Both parents feed the nestlings for a period of 28–30 days, until the young birds fledge.  Each pair raises a single brood a year.

===Feeding===
The wallcreeper is an [[insectivore]], feeding on terrestrial invertebrates, primarily insects and spiders, gleaned from rock faces.<ref name=Handbook>{{cite book |first1=Paul R. |last2=Dobkin |first2=David S. |last3=Wheye |first3=Darryl |last4=Pimm |first4=Stuart L. |date=1994 |title=The Birdwatcher's Handbook |last1=Ehrlich |publisher=Oxford University Press |pages=[https://archive.org/details/birdwatchershand00ehrl/page/440 440] |isbn=978-0-19-858407-0 |url-access=registration |url=https://archive.org/details/birdwatchershand00ehrl/page/440 }}</ref>  It sometimes also chases flying insects in short [[sallying|sallies]] from a rock wall perch. Feeding birds move across a cliff face in short flights and quick hops, often with their wings partially spread.

zh.extract:
红翅旋壁雀（学名：Tichodroma muraria），俗名爬树鸟、石花儿、爬岩树，是雀形目旋壁雀科旋壁雀属的唯一一个物种，舊屬鳾科。
分布于欧洲、西南亚地区以及中国大陆的新疆、西藏、青海、甘肃、宁夏、内蒙古、四川、中国东北、河北、北京、河南、陕西、湖北、江西、安徽、江苏、云南、福建、广东等地，多生活于非树栖高山型、栖息在悬崖和陡坡壁上以及或栖于亚热带常绿阔叶林和针阔混交林带中的山坡壁上。该物种的模式产地在欧洲南部。


分類
本物種的分類在历史上有爭議：在鸟类传统分类系统中，林奈最初把本物種歸入旋木雀科（Certhiidae）。現時基本上確定本屬物種是一個單系群，不過並所有人認同Clements (2007)把本屬獨立成科的主張。Dickinson (2003)認為本屬充其量只能成為鳾科之下的一個亞科（Tichodromadinae）。 


亚种
本物種有兩個亞種，基本上有地域分別：

T. m. muraria：分布於歐洲、西南亞至伊朗北部及西部。
红翅旋壁雀普通亚种（Tichodroma muraria nepalensis）：顏色較深，分布地點以中亞為主，包括有土庫曼斯坦東部，里海以东至帕米尔高原、南抵伊朗東部、阿富汗、东达天山、蒙古，亦見於中国大陆的新疆、西藏、青海、甘肃、宁夏、内蒙古、四川、中国东北、河北、北京、河南、陕西、湖北、江西、安徽、江苏、云南、福建?、广东等地。该物种的模式产地在亚洲中部。


化石紀錄
Tichodroma capeki (來自匈牙利波爾加迪的中新世晚期地層) 


参考文献


參考書目
Clements, James F. (2007) The Clements Checklist of Birds of the World sixth edition
Dickinson, Edward C. (ed) 2003 The Howard and Moore Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World third edition


外部連結

Neveu Frank, Salamandre Films. (ed) 2013. Like a butterfly - The incredible life of the wallcreeper
